title: 'Extraction' and the 10 Best Movies Released During Quarantine
format: Fifty-eight days into lockdown, Sean and Amanda count down the 10 best movies released since quarantine began, a field split between Netflix and HBO docs and indies scrounged off VOD. Then Sean talks to Extraction director Sam Hargrave about stunt work, the 13-minute oner, and going from doubling Captain America to a Netflix debut.

segments.heading: The VOD Revolution
segments.note: how movies are being released two months into lockdown

films.title: Trolls World Tour
films.note: The PVOD test case ($19.99 to rent) that the pair used the prior week to argue the release model is changing.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: Spike Lee's Vietnam film, landing on Netflix June 12. Sean calls it the most significant new Oscar movie of the year, dropped into a pileup with two other releases.

films.title: The King of Staten Island
films.note: Judd Apatow's Pete Davidson film, one of the June-12 home releases Sean gripes about being bunched together.

films.title: Scoob!
films.note: Cited among the studio movies coming straight to homes in May and June.

films.title: Artemis Fowl
films.note: Also on the June-12 pileup of straight-to-home releases.

films.title: The High Note
films.note: Focus Features' Dakota Johnson / Tracee Ellis Ross film going straight to VOD in late May, which Sean calls the clearest signal of where specialty studios are headed.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: The example for how summer movies tend to fade by Oscar season despite strong openings.

films.title: Tigertail
films.note: Alan Yang's Netflix film, raised as a counterpoint that streaming lets filmmakers who don't usually get to make Hollywood movies tell their stories.

films.title: Crip Camp
films.note: The Netflix doc on the disability-rights movement; both agree it's one of the year's best so far, but they've covered it twice already so it's kept off the list.

films.title: Bacurau
films.note: A nether-zone title they'd already discussed, watched via virtual screening. Not on the list, but Sean says go see it.

segments.heading: The 10 Best Movies Released During Quarantine
segments.note: counted down 10 to 1; their No. 10 was the series The Last Dance
segments.subject: movies released since quarantine

films.title: Beastie Boys Story
films.note: No. 1 (Amanda's): the Spike Jonze-directed live document. Amanda idolizes the band and reads it as a study in image management: three men deciding exactly what they'll let you know, off the cuff but from a script.

films.title: Extraction
films.note: No. 2 (Sean's): Sam Hargrave's Netflix action debut, which the two had been mildly dismissive of on an earlier show. Sean recanted after a second watch of the 13-minute oner and the bridge shootout, and after interviewing Hargrave.

films.title: Driveways
films.note: No. 3: Andrew Ahn's quiet film about a mother and son cleaning out a dead relative's house in upstate New York, befriending a widowed vet played by Brian Dennehy in one of his last roles. Wrecked both of them; Sean singles out the child actor and the final six minutes.

films.title: Arkansas
films.note: No. 4: Clark Duke's country-noir directorial debut from a John Brandon novel, with Liam Hemsworth, John Malkovich and Vince Vaughn. Sean thinks it's good, not amazing, but wants a whole episode on what Vaughn is doing lately.

films.title: Bad Education
films.note: No. 5 (Sean's): Cory Finley's HBO film about a Long Island school-embezzlement scandal, from a New York Magazine article, with Hugh Jackman, Allison Janney and Ray Romano. Sean, whose family is deep in Long Island public schools, loved stage-actor Hugh Jackman going for it; Amanda wanted more of the teen journalist.

films.title: The Half of It
films.note: No. 6 (Amanda's): Alice Wu's queer-Cyrano teen comedy for Netflix, with Leah Lewis as Ellie Chu ghost-writing love letters for the jock to the girl they both like. Amanda rates it above the usual Netflix teen fare; Wu's first film in about 15 years.

films.title: Blood Quantum
films.note: No. 7 (Sean's): Jeff Barnaby's gory, inventive zombie movie told from the perspective of the Mi'kmaq, with an all-First-Nations cast and crew. In the Romero lineage; on Shudder. A late add Sean was floored by.

films.title: Natalie Wood: What Remains Behind
films.note: No. 8 (Amanda's): Laurent Bouzereau's HBO doc made with the family's direct involvement, narrated and half-interviewed by daughter Natasha Gregson Wagner. Not objective and not trying to be; both find the leading-question segment with Robert Wagner about Wood's death riveting.

films.title: Spaceship Earth
films.note: No. 9 (Sean's): Matt Wolf's doc on the Biosphere 2 crew who sealed themselves in a biodome in the early '90s. Sean calls it the perfect quarantine movie; Amanda couldn't stand the hippies and thought using natural sun was cheating.

segments.heading: Sam Hargrave on Extraction
segments.note: stunt performer turned coordinator turned director

films.title: Extraction
films.note: His directorial debut, projected by Netflix to reach 90 million viewers. Scouted in India and Bangladesh between Infinity War and Endgame; references were Bullitt and Ronin. The trust-on-the-roof beat mid-oner was improvised on the day; a sequel is officially in the works with Joe Russo writing.

films.title: The Avengers
films.note: Where he first doubled Captain America, under Joss Whedon, the start of the arc he calls a Cinderella story.

films.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest
films.note: His first big set, one of over a hundred stunt performers, proving himself among 30-year legends.

films.title: Captain America: The Winter Soldier
films.note: Where he met the Russos as Cap's stunt double and helped design fights.

films.title: Captain America: Civil War
films.note: The Russos' call to make him stunt coordinator: his jump from double to coordinating on a Marvel scale.

films.title: Avengers: Infinity War
films.note: Given extra second-unit days under Alexander Witt; the Russos started handing him his own footage.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: He directed beloved-character death scenes solo, and came out of retirement to suit up as Cap for the Cap-versus-Cap fight, fighting his own younger brother on camera.

films.title: The Hunger Games
films.note: Among the hundred-million-dollar movies he'd coordinated before Civil War.
The other big-oner movie. He says the crew smacked their heads seeing its featurettes, but it's different enough not to feel derivative.

films.title: The Raid
films.note: One of the touchstones for the feeling he chases in an action sequence.

films.title: John Wick
films.note: Chad Stahelski and David Leitch's world, the bar he says he's always trying to keep up with, having been on their teams.

films.title: The Mask of Zorro
films.note: His last-great-thing pick, Martin Campbell's film, rewatched for structure while writing a script. He calls it nearly perfect: everything driven by character.

films.title: Casino Royale
films.note: Cited alongside Zorro as why his appreciation for Martin Campbell has soared.

referenced.group: The nether zone (late-Feb to mid-March releases)

films: To All the Boys I've Loved Before
